request.question: Which Canadian singer sang " something stronger" in the musical applause?

title: Sweden in the Eurovision Song Contest 1965
Applause, Applause
The Singer Sang His Song
Jumbo (Bee Gees song)
Applause (Bonnie Franklin song)
Anna Russell
Spin It Again! Live in Munich
Leland Palmer (actress)
Jennifer Gillis
Len Cariou
sentences: After a year of absence, Sweden competed in the Eurovision Song Contest 1965 with the opera singer Ingvar Wixell, and the song "Absent Friend" (originally "Annorstädes vals") composed by Dag Wirén.
For the first time, one singer sang all the songs in the national final, and Ingvar was also the first male singer to represent Sweden.
It was the first ESC entry that was not performed in the country's native language; this led to the introduction of the language rule the following year.
Applause, Applause was a Canadian musical variety television miniseries which aired on CBC Television in 1974.
"The Singer Sang His Song" is a song written by Barry, Robin and Maurice Gibb and recorded by the English rock group Bee Gees, released in early 1968 as a single along with Jumbo.
In some countries the song was the B-side of Jumbo but in others they were promoted as a double A-side.
"Jumbo" is a song released by the Bee Gees, written by Barry, Robin & Maurice Gibb.
It was released as a double A side with "The Singer Sang His Song" but featured as the lead track in some territories.

"Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.
The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.

The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.
Anna Russell (born Anna Claudia Russell-Brown; 27 December 191118 October 2006) was an English–Canadian singer and comedian.
She gave many concerts in which she sang and played comic musical sketches on the piano.

Among her best-known works are her concert performances and famous recordings of "The Ring of the Nibelungs (An Analysis)" – a humorous 22-minute synopsis of Richard Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen" – and (on the same album) her parody "How to Write Your Own Gilbert and Sullivan Opera."
Spin It Again!
Live in Munich is a live album and video by the Canadian band Saga.
It was recorded in 2012 at the end of their 20/20 tour and celebrates the reunion with original singer Michael Sadler.
It was also the first Saga release featuring their new drummer Mike Thorne.
Besides three songs from "20/20", it includes mainly songs from the first five Saga albums.
The concert was released on different formats by edel records/earMUSIC, including a double CD, DVD (Super Jewel Box packaging) and Blu-Ray.
The video formats also include a twenty-minute tour documentary.
The CD has edited applause between the main set and the encores, but otherwise the performance is identical.
As is typical for Saga shows in Germany, Michael Sadler does some German announcements.
The concert also includes a litte "unplugged" set.
After returning to the stage, Sadler says "What shall I play?"
He starts on "Images (Chapter 1)", then stops, goes into "No Regrets (Chapter 5)", stops again and finally declares "I got it!"
before launching into "Tired World (Chapter 6)".
Leland Palmer (born June 16, 1945, Port Washington, New York) is an American actress, dancer, and singer who has appeared on stage, in motion pictures, and on television.
She appeared on Broadway in "Bajour", "A Joyful Noise", "Hello, Dolly!
", "Applause", and "Pippin".
Palmer received two Tony Award nominations: in 1967 for featured actress in a musical (Miss Jimmie in "A Joyful Noise"), and in 1973 for actress in a musical (Fastrada in "Pippin").
She appeared as Viola in the rock musical "Your Own Thing" during a highly regarded run at the Huntington Hartford Theater in Los Angeles in August 1968.

Jennifer Kristine Gillis (born 26 April 1996) is a Canadian singer, dancer and actress with an extensive resume in musical theatre, television, singing, radio, recording, and animated voice-over work.

Gillis is most noted for performing in Sir Andrew Lloyd Webber's CBC TV reality show "Over the Rainbow"-a competition to be cast as the leading role of Dorothy Gale in Lloyd Webber's forthcoming production of "The Wizard of Oz" in Toronto, Ontario.
Being the youngest aspiring singer in the competition, she singularly represented her province of British Columbia and as a result was named the Top 6th musical theatre performer in all of Canada.
Since "Over the Rainbow", Gillis sang the Canadian national anthem "O Canada" for the Prime Minister of Canada, Stephen Harper on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, Ontario on Canada Day in 2013.
She aspires to become a Broadway actress someday and wants to dedicate her life to performing.
Leonard Joseph "Len" Cariou (born September 30, 1939) is a Canadian actor, best known for his portrayal of Sweeney Todd in the original cast of "", which earned him a Tony award.
He currently plays the patriarch, Henry Reagan, NYPD Police Commissioner (retired), in the multi-generational television series "Blue Bloods" on CBS.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Which Canadian singer sang " something stronger" in the musical applause?
hop1_docs: Applause, Applause | Applause, Applause was a Canadian musical variety television miniseries which aired on CBC Television in 1974.

Applause (Bonnie Franklin song) | "Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.  The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.  The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.

Siti binti Saad | Siti binti Saad (1880–1950) was a pioneering artist in the taraab genre of east African music.  In an era in which male singers predominated, she was a pioneer as a woman singer in the genre: she was the first woman in East Africa to record her music in an album.  In contrast to previous singers who sang in Arabic, she sang in Swahili.  She sang in cities of the coast of Tanganyika and Zanzibar.

Parichay (singer) | Parichay (born August 22) is an Indo-Canadian singer, composer, and music producer.  He has released three albums and collaborated in Bollywood film songs including a remix of "Yamla Pagla Deewana" and "O Meri Chandni" from the film "Chaar Din Ki Chandni" which he sang with Sunidhi Chauhan.  In 2016, he composed music for the Bollywood film "Loveshhuda".

Applause Cheer Boo Hiss | Applause Cheer Boo Hiss is an EP by Canadian indie rock band Land of Talk, released on April 4, 2006 on Dependent Music in Canada.  It would later be released on Rebel Group Records in the United States on March 20, 2007.

Applause (musical) | Applause is a musical with a book by Betty Comden and Adolph Green, lyrics by Lee Adams, and music by Charles Strouse.  The musical is based on the 1950 film "All About Eve" and the short story on which the movie is based, Mary Orr's "The Wisdom of Eve".  The story centers on aging star Margo Channing, who innocently takes a fledgling actress under her wing, unaware that the ruthless Eve is plotting to steal her career and her man.

Stronger (Carlene Carter album) | Stronger is an album released in 2008 by American country music singer Carlene Carter.  Included is "I'm So Cool," a song originally recorded on Carter's 1980 album, "Musical Shapes".
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Applause (Bonnie Franklin song) [2]: B-side featured Lauren Bacall "performing 'Something Greater' together with Len Cariou." (quote from [2])
  - Applause (musical) [6]: musical title and creative team (book by Betty Comden and Adolph Green; lyrics by Lee Adams; music by Charles Strouse). (cite [6])
  - Parichay [4] and Applause Cheer Boo Hiss [5] mention Canadian/Indo-Canadian artists but are unrelated to the musical Applause. (cite [4], [5])
- Ambiguities:
  - Song-title mismatch: question asks "something stronger" but passage [2] lists "Something Greater" — no "Something Stronger" appears in the provided passages.
  - Performer candidates: only Len Cariou is named alongside the song in [2]; the passages do not label him as Canadian here. Other Canadian-titled artists appear in different passages but are not tied to the musical.
  - Missing expected pages/variants: no passage for a song titled "Something Stronger" or a dedicated "Len Cariou" page in the supplied set.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity: "Len Cariou" (exact token as in passage [2]).
  - Precise missing fact needed: confirmation of the exact song title in the musical's recordings/song list ("Something Greater" vs "Something Stronger") and confirmation that Len Cariou (or another named performer) is a Canadian singer who sang that song in Applause.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Applause (Bonnie Franklin song)" page — check lede/infobox and the single/recording or release details for the B-side and credited performers (verify exact song title and performers).
     - Search keywords: "Applause Bonnie Franklin 'Something Greater' B-side Len Cariou Lauren Bacall".
  2) Open "Len Cariou" page — check lede/infobox for nationality, and "Career"/"Discography"/"Stage credits" sections for his performance of the song or role in Applause.
     - Search keywords: "Len Cariou nationality Canadian 'Something Greater' Applause".
  3) Open "Applause (musical)" page — check song list and original cast/recordings sections for a song titled "Something Greater" or "Something Stronger" and performer credits.
     - Check sections: lede, "Songs"/"Musical numbers", "Original cast"/"Productions".
  4) If needed, open "Lauren Bacall" page or the single-release entry to corroborate she performed the B-side with Len Cariou.
- Note: I will not provide a final answer until those pages/sections are retrieved and confirm the exact song title and the performer's nationality/credit.
hop2_docs: Applause (Bonnie Franklin song) | "Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.  The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.  The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.

Len Cariou | Leonard Joseph "Len" Cariou (born September 30, 1939) is a Canadian actor, best known for his portrayal of Sweeney Todd in the original cast of "", which earned him a Tony award.  He currently plays the patriarch, Henry Reagan, NYPD Police Commissioner (retired), in the multi-generational television series "Blue Bloods" on CBS.

One Man (film) | One Man is a Canadian drama film, released in 1977.  Directed by Robin Spry, the film stars Len Cariou as Jason Brady, a television journalist in Montreal who is investigating a chemical leak from a local factory which has poisoned a number of children.
Applause, Applause | Applause, Applause was a Canadian musical variety television miniseries which aired on CBC Television in 1974.

The Four Seasons (1981 film) | The Four Seasons is a 1981 romantic comedy film written and directed by and starring Alan Alda, co-starring Carol Burnett, Len Cariou, Sandy Dennis, Rita Moreno, Jack Weston and Bess Armstrong.

A Little Night Music (film) | A Little Night Music is a 1977 film adaptation of the musical "A Little Night Music".  It stars Elizabeth Taylor, Diana Rigg, and Lesley-Anne Down.  It also features Len Cariou, Hermione Gingold, and Laurence Guittard who reprised their Broadway roles.  The film was directed by Harold Prince.

Cooper & Hemingway: The True Gen | Cooper & Hemingway: The True Gen is a 2013 documentary film about the 20-year friendship between writer Ernest Hemingway and film actor Gary Cooper.  Written and directed by John Mulholland, it is narrated by actor Sam Waterston with actor Len Cariou as the voice of writer Ernest Hemingway.
response.summary_2: The passages do not specify a song titled "Something Stronger"; they show the B-side was "Something Greater," performed by Lauren Bacall together with Len Cariou. Leonard Joseph "Len" Cariou is identified as a Canadian actor. Lauren Bacall is described as the star of the production who made her musical theatre debut on that B-side.
response.answer: No — there is no "Something Stronger"; the B-side was "Something Greater," sung by Lauren Bacall with Len Cariou (Len Cariou is identified as Canadian).
